This psalm begins with a question that cuts to the heart of every human society: Can we trust those with power to use it justly? David's answer is brutally honest. The rulers and judges—the ones supposed to uphold righteousness—are not deliberating in secret toward justice. They are scheming injustice. They are crushing the vulnerable with violence. The problem is not merely occasional corruption or a few bad decisions. It runs deeper. Their hearts are fundamentally oriented toward wrong. This is a psalm about the erosion of justice itself, about those who should protect the innocent becoming instruments of oppression instead. David isn't making an abstract complaint about corruption. He's describing the lived reality of people suffering under unjust rule, with no earthly court to turn to.

What makes David's accusation even more severe is that he traces this corruption back to its root. These judges and rulers are not simply making poor choices—their wickedness is innate, established from before birth. They are deceivers by their very nature, like serpents with venom in their mouths who refuse to hear even the voice of reason or appeal. This is not a call for moral reformation through better training or policy. This is an acknowledgment that human depravity runs so deep that some people are fundamentally closed to justice. The cobra that plugs its ears refuses the charmer's spell—and these leaders refuse God's call to righteousness. You cannot reason with what refuses to listen.

Because human justice has failed, David appeals directly to God. His prayers are violent and uncompromising: shatter their teeth, tear out their fangs, make them vanish, dissolve them like slugs, let them see no sun. These are not requests for rehabilitation or second chances. They are cries for divine judgment against those who use their power to destroy the innocent. The psalm doesn't shy away from the intensity of this desire for justice. Verse 10 shows the righteous rejoicing and washing their feet in blood—a stark image of vindication so complete that even the sight of the wicked's destruction brings joy. Yet beneath this fury is a fundamental confidence: God sees what rulers do. He judges the earth. He will settle accounts.

The promise that closes this psalm cuts through all our compromises with injustice. When God's judgment comes, humanity will finally acknowledge a truth too long denied: the righteous have a reward, and God is judge. This is not a comfortable meditation. It confronts us with the reality that injustice in the world is real, that human courts fail, and that our only hope lies in trusting that God's justice is certain and final. If you are suffering under the weight of unjust systems, this psalm names your pain. If you benefit from injustice, this psalm warns you. Either way, you cannot escape the judge of all the earth.
